title: Advanced Git Tools
learningObjectives: Use git bisect to find the commit that introduced a regression using binary search
Recover lost work and undo destructive operations with git reflog
Manage multiple working trees simultaneously with git worktree
Use git submodule to vendor external repositories as subdirectories
Understand git bundle for offline and airgapped repository transfer

theory.content:
git bisect — Binary Search for Bugs

When a bug exists in the current code but you don't know which commit introduced it, git bisect automates the search using binary search. You tell Git one known-good commit and one known-bad commit; Git checks out the midpoint and asks you to test. Each answer cuts the search space in half.

# Start a bisect session
git bisect start

# Mark current HEAD as broken
git bisect bad

# Mark a known-good commit (tag, SHA, or ref)
git bisect good v2.0.0

# Git checks out the midpoint — run your tests, then:
git bisect good   # this commit is fine
git bisect bad    # this commit is broken

# When done, return HEAD to original position
git bisect reset

# Automate with a test script (exit 0 = good, non-zero = bad)
git bisect run ./test-script.sh

Time complexity: O(log n). With 1,024 commits between good and bad, bisect needs at most 10 steps to identify the culprit. Automated bisect with git bisect run requires a script that exits 0 for a good build and non-zero for a bad one — Git marks the result automatically and continues without human input.

git reflog — Your Safety Net

The reflog (reference log) records every position HEAD has occupied: commits, merges, rebases, checkouts, resets, and cherry-picks. Even if a branch is deleted or a rebase goes wrong, the commits are still in the object store and visible in the reflog — for up to 90 days by default.

# View the HEAD reflog
git reflog

# View reflog for a specific branch
git reflog refs/heads/main

# Checkout a specific reflog position
git checkout HEAD@{3}

# Reset hard to a reflog position (recover from bad rebase)
git reset --hard HEAD@{3}

# Recover a deleted branch (find its SHA in reflog first)
git reflog | grep 'commit: Last commit on deleted-branch'
git checkout -b recovered-branch SHA

Common recovery pattern: if a rebase goes wrong, run git reflog, identify the SHA of HEAD just before the rebase began (it will be labeled rebase (start) or you can look for your pre-rebase commit message), then git reset --hard SHA to restore. Reflog entries are local only — they are not pushed to remotes and not included in a git clone.

git worktree — Multiple Checkouts

git worktree lets you check out multiple branches simultaneously into different directories — all sharing the same .git object store, so they don't duplicate history or objects. This is faster and lighter than cloning.

# Add a new worktree for an existing branch
git worktree add ../hotfix-tree hotfix/v2.1

# Create a new branch in a new worktree in one step
git worktree add -b feature/experiment ../experiment-tree

# List all worktrees
git worktree list

# Remove a worktree when done
git worktree remove ../hotfix-tree

# Prune stale worktree references
git worktree prune

Use cases: reviewing a PR while staying on your current branch, running a long test suite in one worktree while developing in another, building release artifacts from main while fixing bugs on a hotfix branch. One constraint: the same branch cannot be checked out in two worktrees simultaneously.

git submodule

git submodule embeds another Git repository as a subdirectory of the parent repo. The parent repo stores only a pointer (the commit SHA of the submodule) — not the submodule's files. The .gitmodules file maps submodule paths to their remote URLs.

# Add a submodule
git submodule add https://github.com/org/library.git vendor/library

# Clone a repo including all submodules
git clone --recurse-submodules https://github.com/org/myproject.git

# Initialize and fetch submodules after a plain clone
git submodule update --init --recursive

# Update all submodules to their latest remote commit
git submodule foreach git pull origin main

# Update the pinned SHA for a submodule
cd vendor/library && git checkout v3.2.0 && cd ..
git add vendor/library && git commit -m "chore: bump library to v3.2.0"

Gotchas: the submodule pointer is pinned to a specific SHA; updating requires explicit steps and a commit in the parent repo. For simpler vendoring consider git subtree (git subtree add --prefix=vendor/lib URL main --squash), which merges the external history directly and requires no special submodule commands for contributors.

git bundle

git bundle packs a repository's history into a single portable file, suitable for offline or airgapped transfer (USB drive, email attachment, S3 object).

# Bundle everything (all branches and tags)
git bundle create repo.bundle --all

# Bundle only the main branch since a specific tag
git bundle create incremental.bundle main ^v2.0.0

# Verify a bundle file before using it
git bundle verify repo.bundle

# Clone from a bundle
git clone repo.bundle my-repo

# Fetch from a bundle into an existing repo
git fetch repo.bundle main:refs/remotes/bundle/main

New in Git 2.49: git bundle create --stdin reads the list of objects from stdin, enabling scripted workflows that compute the object list programmatically before bundling.

git-bisect reference → | git-reflog reference → | git-worktree reference → | git-submodule reference →
